skill.name: agent-browser
skill.description: Automates browser sessions through inference.sh with navigation, element refs, form interaction, screenshots, video recording, proxy settings, file upload, and page JavaScript execution.
skill.summary: Control browser sessions for web testing, capture, and data extraction through inference.sh.
skill.author: inference-sh-8

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many command, network, filesystem, and credential patterns. Most Markdown backtick and hardcoded URL hits are documentation examples, but the skill still exposes high-risk browser automation capabilities, including a remote shell installer, authenticated workflows, cookie extraction, JavaScript execution, screenshots, video, file upload, and proxy routing. No prompt injection or confirmed malicious intent was found, so this is not blocked, but it should not publish without strong warnings and review.

high_findings.title: Remote Installer Piped To Shell
high_findings.description: The quick start instructs users to download a remote script and pipe it directly to sh before login. This is a supply-chain risk because the fetched installer executes immediately if the endpoint, TLS path, or script is compromised. The later checksum note reduces risk but does not protect the pipe-to-shell command itself.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The pipe-to-shell command is explicit on SKILL.md line 17. The surrounding install note confirms this is an intended installation path, not a scanner artifact.

high_findings.title: Credential And Cookie Exposure Workflows
high_findings.description: The skill documents login automation, credential filling, and cookie extraction. This is legitimate for browser automation, but it creates a high risk of exposing session cookies, usernames, passwords, or authenticated page data through command output, screenshots, files, or downstream tools.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documentation explicitly fills password fields and prints cookies extracted with JavaScript. The behavior is user-directed, but the sensitive-data exposure risk is direct.

high_findings.title: Combined Browser Control, Network Access, And Page Execution
high_findings.description: The skill can navigate arbitrary URLs, interact with forms, capture screenshots, upload files, and execute page JavaScript through the inference.sh CLI. This combination can collect or submit sensitive data if used on private or authenticated sites.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command reference clearly lists URL navigation, proxy credentials, file upload paths, and arbitrary page JavaScript. These are expected features but form a high-risk capability set.

medium_findings.title: Proxy Routing Can Bypass Network Assumptions
medium_findings.description: The proxy documentation supports HTTP, HTTPS, and SOCKS5 routing. Proxy support is useful for testing, but it can hide traffic origin, bypass regional controls, or route sensitive sessions through untrusted infrastructure.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: SOCKS5 and other proxy examples are explicitly documented. The risk depends on user configuration, so this is a medium concern rather than confirmed abuse.

medium_findings.title: Screenshots, Video, And Extracted Page Data Saved To Files
medium_findings.description: The capture workflow saves screenshots, element text, body text, links, and optional video files. This is useful for audits and research, but it can persist private page contents on disk if used with sensitive pages.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The template writes captured browser outputs to local files and the video guide enables session recording. The data sensitivity depends on the target website.

medium_findings.title: Shell Templates Execute External CLI Commands With User Input
medium_findings.description: The shell templates pass user-supplied URLs and output paths into infsh commands. The scripts use strict shell settings and quote key variables, but they still run external browser automation based on user input.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The scripts clearly run infsh with user-provided URLs. Quoting and use of a single CLI reduce command-injection evidence, so the remaining concern is controlled external execution.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misclassified As Ruby Execution
low_findings.description: Many static external-command findings come from Markdown code fences and inline command examples. These are instructional examples rather than hidden Ruby backtick execution in application code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are Markdown command examples and fenced documentation. No Ruby source file or runtime backtick execution was found in those locations.

low_findings.title: Relative Documentation Links Misclassified As Path Traversal
low_findings.description: The path traversal findings point to relative Markdown links such as ../SKILL.md. These links navigate documentation and do not read arbitrary filesystem paths at runtime.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The evidence is documentation linking to another file in the skill package. There is no path concatenation or filesystem access in these Markdown links.

low_findings.title: SHA-256 Mention Misclassified As Weak Cryptography
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography findings include documentation about SHA-256 checksum verification. SHA-256 checksums are not a weak algorithm in this context, although checksums do not remove the risk of piping an installer to a shell.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited text describes checksum verification for a CLI installer. This is not evidence of weak cryptographic use in skill code.

dangerous_patterns.title: Pipe To Shell Installation
dangerous_patterns.description: Remote installer execution appears in the quick start and should be replaced with manual download and checksum verification instructions before publication.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command directly pipes a remote HTTPS response into sh. This is a known risky installation pattern.

dangerous_patterns.title: Arbitrary Page JavaScript Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: The execute function accepts a code string and runs it in the current page. This can extract page data, cookies visible to JavaScript, or DOM content from sensitive sessions.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The reference explicitly documents a code field for JavaScript and shows document.cookie extraction. This is a real browser-context execution capability.

dangerous_patterns.title: Browser Capture And Recording Of User Sessions
dangerous_patterns.description: Screenshots and videos can record private data from pages. The capability is expected for this skill but requires strong user consent and secure handling.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command reference and video guide both document visual capture. The risk depends on the sensitivity of the pages being automated.

content.user_title: Automate Browser Tasks With Agent Browser
content.value_statement: Browser testing and web capture often require repeatable clicks, form fills, screenshots, and page extraction. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a structured browser workflow through inference.sh.
actual_capabilities: Open browser sessions and navigate to a target URL through the infsh CLI.
Use @e element refs to click, fill, select, hover, drag, scroll, and upload files.
Refresh snapshots after page changes to get updated URL, title, elements, and screenshots.
Capture viewport or full-page screenshots and optionally record session video.
Execute JavaScript in the current page to extract text, links, counts, or DOM state.
Configure viewport size, user agent, proxy URL, and proxy credentials for a session.
limitations: Requires the inference.sh CLI, account login, and network access to the service.
Element refs become invalid after navigation or dynamic page updates and need new snapshots.
Browser automation can expose credentials, cookies, screenshots, and private page content if misused.
The skill does not include policy checks for target website terms, consent, or rate limits.

use_cases.title: Validate Web Forms
use_cases.description: Run repeatable form discovery, field filling, submission, and result verification for staging or internal applications.

use_cases.title: Capture Research Pages
use_cases.description: Collect page text, links, screenshots, and optional video evidence from public pages for review workflows.

use_cases.title: Debug Authenticated Flows
use_cases.description: Walk through login, dashboard navigation, screenshots, and post-login checks while keeping session state in one browser session.

prompt_templates.title: Open And Inspect A Page
prompt_templates.prompt: Open this URL with agent-browser, summarize the page title, visible text, and available @e element refs: [URL].

prompt_templates.title: Fill A Public Form
prompt_templates.prompt: Use agent-browser to inspect this form, identify the field refs, fill the provided non-sensitive test values, submit it, and report the final page state: [URL].

prompt_templates.title: Capture Page Evidence
prompt_templates.prompt: Use agent-browser to capture a full-page screenshot, extract the main visible text and links, and note any missing or blocked content: [URL].

prompt_templates.title: Test A Multi-Step Flow
prompt_templates.prompt: Use agent-browser to run this authorized multi-step flow, re-snapshot after each navigation, record important screenshots, and avoid printing secrets: [FLOW DETAILS].

output_examples.input: Inspect a product page and capture a full-page screenshot.
output_examples.output: The result includes the page title, current URL, visible interaction refs, a saved full-page screenshot, and a short summary of key page sections.

output_examples.input: Run a contact form test with sample data.
output_examples.output: The result identifies form field refs, confirms each fill action, records the submit action, and reports whether the final page shows success or an error.

output_examples.input: Extract links from a documentation page.
output_examples.output: The result lists visible links with nearby text, notes duplicate or empty labels, and includes the page screenshot for review.

best_practices: Use only authorized sites and non-sensitive test accounts unless explicit permission exists.
Re-snapshot after clicks, form submissions, route changes, and dynamic content loading.
Close every browser session and store screenshots, videos, cookies, and logs securely.
anti_patterns: Do not pipe remote installers to a shell in automated workflows without independent verification.
Do not print passwords, cookies, tokens, or authenticated page data into shared logs.
Do not use proxies, file uploads, or JavaScript execution to bypass website rules or access controls.

faq.question: Does this skill require an external service?
faq.answer: Yes. It uses the inference.sh CLI and service to run browser automation sessions.

faq.question: Can it interact with forms and buttons?
faq.answer: Yes. It uses @e element refs for clicks, fills, selections, checks, drags, scrolling, and uploads.

faq.question: Can it capture screenshots or video?
faq.answer: Yes. It can capture viewport or full-page screenshots and can record video when enabled.

faq.question: Can it run JavaScript on a page?
faq.answer: Yes. The execute function runs JavaScript in the current page context and returns the result.

faq.question: Is it safe for passwords and cookies?
faq.answer: Use caution. The skill can handle credentials and extract cookies, so avoid shared logs and protect all outputs.

faq.question: Why is this report marked high risk?
faq.answer: It combines remote installation, browser control, authenticated workflows, page capture, JavaScript execution, file upload, and proxy support.
